revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23986660476|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:人見神社]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Hitomi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:人見神社 拝殿.jpg | location = 892 Hitomi, Kimitsu City, Chiba Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.337139|N|139.867222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|qid=Q2842779|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}; {{ill|Kamimusubi|qid=Q3275901|1=Kamimusubi}}
| shrine_Rank = Former Gō-sha (village shrine) | founded = Traditional: Reign of Emperor Kōtoku, 940 CE (Tengyō 3) | associated = | annual_festival = | prefecture= {{ill|Kimitsu|qid=Q637928|1=Kimitsu}}; {{ill|Q18338987|qid=Q18338987|1=Q18338987}} }}
'''Hitomi Shrine''' (ひとみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Kimitsu City]], {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, Japan.
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine venerates the following deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * [[Takamimusubi]] * [[Kamimusubi]]
== History == Hitomi Shrine is situated atop Mount Hitomi, overlooking the {{ill|Tokyo Bay|en|Tokyo Bay|qid=Q141017|lt=Tokyo Bay|1=Tokyo Bay}} and near the mouth of the {{ill|Koito River|en|Koito River|qid=Q6426373|lt=Koito River|1=Koito River}}. It has long been revered as one of the "Six Myōken Shrines of Sōshū," which include the upper regions of Kazusa Province (Hitomi Shrine, [[Kururi Shrine]], and Yokota Shrine) and Shimōsa Province ([[Chiba Shrine]], Iitaka Shrine, and Inzai Myōken Shrine).<ref name="yuisho">[https://hitomi-jinja.jp/gosaijin-goyuisyo/ Shrine History] - Hitomi Shrine Official Website</ref><ref name="kanko">[https://www.city.kimitsu.lg.jp/site/kanko/2253.html Hitomi Shrine] - Kimitsu City Official Tourism Page</ref>
According to the "Myōken Engi" from the shrine's affiliated temple, Shōren-ji, the shrine was established during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} under Emperor Kōtoku. Another legend tells of a Myōken statue discovered in nearby Ōhori by a local farmer, Tayuemon, and subsequently enshrined on Mount Hitomi with the help of his companion, Ichiroemon.<ref name="yuisho"/>
In 940 CE (Tengyō 3), during the tenure of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|en|Taira no Tadatsune|qid=Q1105844|lt=Taira no Tadatsune|1=Taira no Tadatsune}} as governor of Kazusa Province, the shrine's Myōken deity was transferred from the {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} to various locations in Kazusa and Shimōsa, with Hitomi Shrine becoming one of the representative shrines. Tadatsune’s grandfather, {{ill|Taira no Yoshifumi|en|Taira no Yoshifumi|qid=Q11483528|lt=Taira no Yoshifumi|1=Taira no Yoshifumi}}, is believed to have been a devoted follower of Myōken and played a key role in the spread of Myōken worship in the eastern provinces.<ref name="yuisho"/>
In 1180 (Jishō 4), [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], after his defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Ishibashiyama|en|Battle of Ishibashiyama|qid=Q2888010|lt=Battle of Ishibashiyama|1=Battle of Ishibashiyama}}, stopped at the shrine while traveling along the inner coast and offered a prayer for military success.
The shrine received numerous dedications over the centuries, including a grant of rice land worth five koku from {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} in 1591 (Tenshō 19) and a ceremonial sword from Ogasawara Hikotayu, the local domain lord, in 1691 (Genroku 4). During the late Edo period, the shrine continued to receive patronage from the Ogasawara family and the local parishioners.<ref name="yuisho"/>
In the Meiji era, as part of the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) reforms, the shrine was renamed Hitomi Shrine, and its Myōken deity was moved to a separate Kannon Hall.
== Shrine Grounds == <gallery> File:Hitomijinja2.jpg|Torii gate File:Hitomijinja3.jpg|Kannon Hall File:Hitomijinja5.jpg|Chōzuya (Purification fountain) File:Hitomijinja4.jpg|View from the shrine (One of the "100 Views of Chiba") </gallery>
== festivals == On its main festival day, the shrine hosts the **Sacred Horse Dedication Ceremony**. In the morning, a sacred horse arrives at the base of the stone steps and, following the mikoshi (portable shrine), races up the steps into the worship hall. The horse is then led around the shrine, first in a clockwise (eastern) direction and then counterclockwise (western), before being tied to the "Turtle Stone." A successful clockwise circuit is considered an omen of a good harvest. After the dedication, the procession continues to the ritual beach ceremony known as ''Ohama-iri'' (御浜出).
== Access == * Approximately 1 minute on foot from the "Hitomi Shrine" stop on the Kimitsu City Community Bus (Hitomi–Ōwada–Kamido Line, Outer Loop). * Closest railway station: [[JR Uchibō Line]], {{ill|Kimitsu Station|en|Kimitsu Station|qid=Q575422|lt=Kimitsu Station|1=Kimitsu Station}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://hitomi-jinja.jp/ Hitomi Shrine Official Website]
{{shinto-stub}}
